coredata.dc:description: The development of carbon dots (CDs) as a facile high luminescent sensor for the detection of heavy metal ions is gaining more attention due to their excellent optical properties and inherent non-toxic nature. Herein, we developed nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using a single-step rapid microwave technique by employing citric acid with L-glutamine followed by boron functionalization using boric acid for the synthesis of boron-nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) by using reflux reaction condition. These BN-CDs exhibited outstanding photo-stability, excellent aqueous dispersibility, low cytotoxicity, and high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of ∼ 21.9 ± 2.7 % and emitted green fluorescence under UV light. BN-CDs were found to display photoluminescence quenching very specific towards auric ions (Au3+ ions) with a detection limit of 3.1 ± 0.45 nM. The quenching observed in the detection process was primarily due to coordination-induced aggregation mechanism. Moreover, BN-CDs were also found to have superior radical scavenging activity, excellent cytocompatibility and cell imaging properties. Thus, prepared BN-CDs can have potential applications in the accurate detection of auric ions as well as in cell imaging applications.

head.ce:title: Facile and green synthesis of highly fluorescent boron and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) for the fluorimetric detection of Au3+ ions in aqueous media and in in vitro cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of carbon dots (CDs) as a facile high luminescent sensor for the detection of heavy metal ions is gaining more attention due to their excellent optical properties and inherent non-toxic nature. Herein, we developed nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using a single-step rapid microwave technique by employing citric acid with L-glutamine followed by boron functionalization using boric acid for the synthesis of boron-nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) by using reflux reaction condition. These BN-CDs exhibited outstanding photo-stability, excellent aqueous dispersibility, low cytotoxicity, and high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of ∼ 21.9 ± 2.7 % and emitted green fluorescence under UV light. BN-CDs were found to display photoluminescence quenching very specific towards auric ions (Au3+ ions) with a detection limit of 3.1 ± 0.45 nM. The quenching observed in the detection process was primarily due to coordination-induced aggregation mechanism. Moreover, BN-CDs were also found to have superior radical scavenging activity, excellent cytocompatibility and cell imaging properties. Thus, prepared BN-CDs can have potential applications in the accurate detection of auric ions as well as in cell imaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are known to cause severe adverse effects on the health of human, fauna, flora and the environment as a whole even in low concentrations above a threshold. Therefore, the detection of these metal ions with high selectivity and sensitivity is of great importance. Nowadays, large amounts of metal ions are enormously released as a result of the rapid urbanization and industrialization, which had a significant hazardous impact on human health and the environment [38,57,61]. According to a United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) report, nearly 40,000–80,000 people/year suffer from toxic metal poisoning as a result of the consumption of contaminated metals [15]. Among them, auric ions are one of the hazardous pollutants that can cause serious ecological toxicity and health disorders [16]. The significance of Au3+ ion detection has grown in tandem with the rising levels of gold salts and nanoparticles (NPs) in wastewater. Both the pharmaceutical and catalysis industries make substantial use of gold compounds. Gold-based medications are utilized as treatments for a variety of diseases, including cancer, HIV, asthma, and malaria. Although metallic gold has a high degree of biocompatibility, its ionic forms, Au3+ and Au+, which are produced in-situ when specific oxidants are present in biological and environmental systems, have a fatal effect on human life [48]. Moreover, the presence of gold species in some of the Ayurvedic / Siddha medicine formulations is sometimes not labelled in medications, and the amount of unreacted Au3+/Au+ in reaction mixtures is not typically known and not reported. Gold species in NPs or ionic forms are detected using analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [28,53]. However, above-mentioned techniques need tedious sample preparation, complex instruments, and are expensive [23,40]. On the contrary, fluorescence detection techniques have distinctive benefits of rapidity, sensitivity, simplicity, easy operation, and low cost. Unfortunately, there are only a very few literature available for fluorimetric detection of Au3+ ions. Most of them use organic fluorophore-based probes; those often need complicated synthetic procedures, drawn-out reaction times, poor optical properties and detection limits (high LoD). Furthermore, most of these fluorescent sensors reported for the detection of auric ions was in organic or aqueous-organic conditions which was not suitable for the detection of contaminants in biological systems [1,21]. Even though quantum dots (QDs) were employed for the detection of toxic contaminants, the toxicity of these semi-conductor-based QDs was considered to be a major limitation.

Carbon dots (CDs) are found to overcome this limitation with their comparable optical properties as that of the QDs with excellent biocompatibility. Although CDs have been widely employed as sensors for heavy metals like Fe3+ [62], Cu2+ [47], Hg2+ [37], Ag+ [31], there are a very few reports on Au3+ ions sensing using CDs [1,21]. An unusual form of carbon, carbon dots have a spherical shape and diameters below 10 nm, and contain sp2/sp3 carbon domains with an amorphous nature [9,34]. Several methods such as pyrolysis, laser ablation [19,4], hydrothermal treatment [2,49], chemical oxidation [52], and microwave irradiation [48] are employed for the fabrication of carbon dots. Among these methods, microwave-based methods are faster and less labor-intensive. Also, the quantum yield and fluorescence capabilities of CDs are reported to be enhanced by doping with heteroatoms and subjecting them to surface passivation [10]. Among the many dopants, boron atoms are efficiently used as a dopant because the boron atom is adjacent to the carbon atom in the periodic table so the atomic radii are similar to the carbon atom. Because of the structural similarity and physicochemical properties, the boron doping will enhance the defects of CDs. The energy defects and active sites are induced by doping the low electronegativity of B atoms with CDs. Also, the electron-deficient state of the B atom causes surface defects, resulting in changes in the emissive properties of CDs and increases the optical properties [18]. The fluorescence properties of the carbon dots are enhanced by boron doping because it alters the arrangements of the carbon and nitrogen atoms as well as the surface functional groups [27]. It is desirable to possess a reliable sensor with excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and higher quantum yield for detecting Au3+ ions. The analyte-CDs interaction plays an important role in the determination of changes that occur in CDs’ inherent fluorescence characteristics such as changes in fluorescence intensity and shifts in emission wavelength. These changes are utilized for the sensitive and targeted monitoring of the analyte [7]. There are a few CDs reported for the detection of auric ions, however, most of them are synthesized using harsh conditions such as using acids and toxic solvents. This dearth of green and cheaper methods for auric ions detection motivated us to start this study. Recently, there is an increased focus on the sustainability aspects of any new technology being developed. In this paper, we too have addressed this aspect for the synthesis of CDs. We used green chemistry and processes to synthesize our carbon dots. We used aqueous chemistry, as well as cheaper toxin-free green chemicals and solvents to synthesize our CDs. In this study, anhydrous citric acid was utilized as the carbon source, and L-glutamine was used as the surface passivation agent to produce highly luminescent, water-soluble carbon dots (N-CDs). Moreover, we choose microwave method as our process to synthesize CDs as it is considered a green and less-energy intensive process for nanomaterial synthesis [41].

Herein, we developed a novel fluorescent sensor for auric ion detection. The new synthetic strategy exploits the development of a fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) followed by boron functionalization with L-glutamine and citric acid in a microwave oven. Microscopic and spectroscopic methods have been performed extensively on the resulting BN-CDs for the determination of the morphology and surface functional groups. The synthesized BN-CDs, which exhibited good photoluminescence, could function as an effective sensor for selective auric ion detection. The synthesized fluorescent probe showed great selectivity and sensitivity for Au3+ detection in a variety of analyte concentrations. To test the sensor’s practical applicability, samples of river water, seawater, and tap water were successfully analyzed for Au3+ ions. Also, plausible fluorescence quenching mechanisms of BN-CDs in the presence of Au3+ ions were evaluated and reported with evidence. In future with further research, the present sensitive and selective detection technique using BN-CDs can be further extendable for Au3+ ions detection in on-site field applications.

Metal salts of Au3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Zr2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, Fe3+, and Fe2+ were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Bangalore, India. Ethanol, Methanol, Phosphate buffer solution (PBS), DMSO (Dimethyl sulfoxide), H2SO4 (Sulphuric acid), and Quinine Sulphate were purchased from SD-Fine Chemical Limited, Mumbai, India. DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium), DPPH (2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl), Live dead assay dyes (ethidium bromide and calcein), and MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) were purchased from HiMedia Laboratories Private Limited, Bombay, India. All of the solutions were prepared using de-ionized water. All measurements were performed using phosphate-buffered saline solution (PBS) at pH 7.

The N-CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method. Briefly, 0.275 g of L-glutamine and 0.75 g of citric acid were dissolved in 5 ml of de-ionized water. The solution was stirred for 10 minutes; the resulting solution was kept in a microwave oven at 600 watts for 3 minutes, as explained in the schematic shown in Fig. 1. This resulted in the formation of brown-colored residue. The residue was then brought to room temperature and 10 ml of de-ionized water was added and stirred at room temperature to obtain a brown-colored solution. The final solution was filtered using a Whatman filter paper (Diameter - 125 mm, Pore size - 12–25 μm) after being centrifuged for 12 minutes at 10,000 rpm to eliminate the large-size particles. The supernatant was lyophilized and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further characterization [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BN-CDs via boric acid-mediated doping of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A known quantity of N-CDs (0.1 mg) was taken and dispersed in 5 ml of de-ionized water. To this, 0.124 g of boric acid was added and kept in a reflux condition at 60 °C for 24 hours. The combined mixture was then allowed to cool at room temperature overnight and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 minutes. The brown solution was collected and labelled as BN-CDs [41].

The PL spectrum was observed at room temperature using BN-CDs with different metal ions. Briefly, different metal ions (100 μM, 100 μL) were added to 100 μL of BN-CDs (0.1 mg/ml), and to that 400 μL of PBS was added and made upto 3 ml using distilled water. The photoluminescence spectrum was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength of 430 nm.

The PL quenching experiments were conducted at room temperature using BN-CDs and auric ions. Briefly, 100 μL of the varied concentrations (0 μM to 40 μM) of auric ions were taken, and to that 100 μL of BN-CDs (0.1 mg/ml), 400 μL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 7) was added and made upto 3 ml using distilled water. The PL was measured with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength of 430 nm. The calibration curve was constructed using auric ions concentration and fluorescence intensity. The analysis of auric ions in real water samples was done using this calibration curve. The detection limit was computed using the equation.
(1) LOD = 3.3 (σ/S)
Where the standard deviation of the sample is represented as ‘σ’, the slope of the curve is represented as ‘S’.

BN-CDs’ antioxidant potential was assessed using the DPPH assay as per protocol reported (37). The DPPH reagent was prepared by taking 1 mg of DPPH (2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) in 20 ml of methanol. The BN-CDs were prepared in various working concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/ml) using a stock concentration of 1 mg/ml. The 50 μL of the aqueous BN-CDs were taken in a 96-well microtiter plate followed by addition of 150 μL of DPPH reagent and incubated for 30 minutes at 37 °C in the dark condition. The absorbance of the sample was read at 517 nm [59]. The following formula was utilized to estimate the percentage of radical scavenging of BN-CDs.
(2) Radical scavenging (%) = [(Ac-As) ÷ Ac]× 100
Where,
Ac - Absorbance of control and
As - Absorbance of sample.
To determine the radical scavenging activity of BN-CDs, ascorbic acid (AA) was used as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity measurement and live cell imaging

ce:section.ce:para: The NIH-3T3 (mouse fibroblast cell line) was cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) high glucose medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotic solution (penicillin-streptomycin) and maintained in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C [22].

The in vitro cell viability of BN-CDs on NIH-3T3 was investigated using the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) method. Briefly, the cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well in a 96-well plate and incubated at a temperature of 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 24 hours. The samples were prepared at different concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 75,100 µg/ml) using DMEM without FBS. Once the cells reached 80 % confluency, the media with FBS was removed and the samples were added to the cells and incubated for 6 hours. After incubation, the samples were removed and 10 μL of MTT (1 mg/ml) solution was added to each well and incubated for an additional 4 hours under the same condition. After that, 190 μL of DMSO was added to fully dissolve the formazan crystals, and the absorbance of each well was read at 570 nm using a Tecan multi-plate reader (Magellan-Infinite 200 PRO). The below-given formula was employed to calculate the cell viability percentage,
(3) % of cell viability = [As ÷ Ac] × 100
Where,
As - the absorbance of treated samples and
Ac - the absorbance of cell control.

To further visualize the viable cells, NIH-3T3 cells were seeded in a 48-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well and incubated in a 5 % CO2 at a temperature of 37 °C for 24 hours. The cells were then incubated with BN-CDs at different concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 100 µg/ml) and incubated for 6 hours. Post-treatment, the cells were stained with 20 µL of ethidium homodimer (λex = 524 nm and λem= 617 nm) and 5 µL of calcein-AM (λex = 494 nm and λem= 528 nm) prepared in serum-free media and incubated at dark conditions at room temperature for 30 minutes. After incubation, the cells were imaged using an Olympus confocal FV 3000 microscope [22,24].

The NIH-3T3 cells was seeded onto 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells / well and incubated at 37 °C, in a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere. When the confluence of cells reached 80 %, BN-CDs (prepared in serum-free media at a concentration of 0.1 mgmL− 1) were added to cells. After incubation for 6 hours, BN-CDs uptaken were imaged using an EVOS fluorescence microscope [17].

Fig. 2a shows the typical HR-TEM micrograph of BN-CDs which reveals the abundance of monodisperse quasi-spherical nanoparticles. Similar quasi-spherical nanostructures have been reported in other CDs papers in the literature. The average size of the particles was found to be 5.2 ± 0.7 nm as shown in Fig. 2b. The zeta potential value was determined to be –13.0 ± 3 mV, as shown in Fig. 2c.

The amorphous nature of the as-prepared BN-CDs is confirmed by an XRD analysis. The XRD spectrum showed a broad peak at 2θ = 25° which is attributed to the 002 Bragg reflection as shown in Fig. 2d. This broad peak was linked to the proliferating sp3 defects in carbon-based materials [14]. A poor crystallization and amorphous nature of BN-CDs are indicated by the interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm for BN-CDs, which is greater than 0.33 nm for bulk graphite [41]. Similar results were reported in the literature for other carbon dots with amorphous nature [3,6,32,42,45,49,51]. XRD results indicate that boron and nitrogen doping does not influence the disordered carbon structures of BN-CDs.

Raman spectroscopy was used to determine the material's characteristic disorder by determining its amorphous/crystalline nature. The D and G bands in the Raman spectrum correspond to the disordered structure of carbon materials [36]. The Raman spectrum of BN-CDs as seen from Fig. 2e has two peaks positioned at 1331 and 1575 cm−1 that correspond to D and G bands, respectively. The carbon atom vibration with pendent bonds in the terminal plane of glassy carbon or disordered graphite is explained by the D band (A1g) mode. The vibrations of the sp2-bonded carbon atom connected to graphite in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice are explained by the G band (E2g) mode. The extent of the defect of B/N-CDs was determined by analyzing the peak position and the intensity of the D and G bands (ID/IG). The calculated ID/IG ratio (the ratio between the integrated area of the D peak and that of the G peak) is 1.07, which is the characteristic disorder extent of the sp3 /sp2 carbon domain. This indicates the high degree of defects formed by amination could disrupt regular graphitic structure [39].

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to obtain the different surface functional groups present in BN-CDs. Comparison of FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs with BN-CDs is shown in Fig. 3. A wide peak at 3192 cm−1 for BN-CDs is indicative of amine and hydroxyl (OH) functionalities confirming the successful transfer of the amino group from L-glutamine to the BN-CDs. It was reported in the literature that OH groups are crucial for CDs’ emission due to their good electron-donating nature. The photoluminescence is enhanced with increasing number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs [58]. The presence of an amino group peak at 1173 cm−1 corresponds to C-N Vibrations. The signal at 1695 cm−1 corresponds to carbonyl functionality or CO vibrations, whereas the band at 2287 cm−1 indicates the existence of a carboxyl group on the surface of the BN-CDs through the functionalization of boric acid. The CH stretching vibration was identified by the presence of bands at 1408 cm−1 confirming the presence of an alkyl group in the BN-CDs. The narrow band at around 1188 cm−1 is indicative of the B-O3/B-O-B/C-N stretching vibrations, confirming the interaction of boric acid with N-CDs [33]. Around 879 cm−1, a small peak was noticed, which is consistent with B-C stretching in the BN-CDs. At 540 cm−1, a carbonization peak was noted.

Carbon dots’ surface functional groups influence their emission properties [60]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is a quantitative technique to determine the target materials’ elemental state, chemical composition and binding energy of the material of interest. XPS was used to determine the different elemental ratios present in the surface of the CDs by deconvoluting the core level peaks. The heteroatoms nitrogen, boron, and oxygen are linked to the carbon moiety in N-CDs. The BN-CDs exhibited distinct peaks for C (26.59 %), O (55.72 %), N (2.55 %), and B (15.15 %), as demonstrated in Fig. 4a. A thorough peak-fitting examination of the synthesized BN-CDs. C1s peak is displayed in Fig. 4b. The six distinct carbon bonding states CC (284.77 eV), C−N (285.70 eV), C−O (286.75 eV), and CO (289.33 eV) were quantitatively distinguished using a high-resolution C1s spectrum that was fitted. A peak corresponding to the π-π* shake-up satellite was shown at 290.6 eV. The three deconvoluted peaks at 195.20, 194.04, and 192.72 eV provide high-resolution B1s spectra, which, as illustrated in Fig. 4c, confirm the B-CO2, B2O3, and B-O bond, respectively. Fig. 4d shows the high-resolution O1s spectra of the BN-CDs. The six distinct bonding states of oxygen: B−O/CO (531.93 eV), C-O (532.75 eV), B2O3 (533.59 eV), C-OH (534.33 eV), COO- (535.14 eV), and OH (536.28 eV) were shown in the spectrum. As in Fig. 4e, a high-resolution N1s spectrum is explained from the two deconvoluted peaks at 400.98 and 402.48 eV, confirming the -NH and C-N bond respectively. Thus, the XPS spectra verified the presence of the amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups in BN-CDs and also revealed the different binding sites that were available in the synthesized BN-CDs. This result was found to be in coherence with FTIR results as well as consistent with the previous literature [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on optical properties of the BN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties were studied using the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The purified BN-CDs solution (brown) obtained displayed green emissions under UV radiation (at 365 nm) as shown in Fig. 5a. The peak maxima observed at 290 nm is attributed to the π- π* transition of aromatic CO bonds in the core and at 321 nm to the n- π* transition of CC bonds due to heteroatom surface functional group. Fig. 5b explains the PL spectra of excitation-dependent emission spectra which were further investigated for the photophysical properties of the BN-CDs. As expected for any CDs, BN-CDs showed excitation-dependent emission behavior as depicted in Fig. 5c. There was an increase in the PL intensity upon increasing the excitation wavelength (λex) from 290 to 350 nm. Further increase in λex resulted in the gradual drop of PL intensity. The excitation-dependent emission behaviour is due to the quantum confinement effect, surface traps [8], and the electronegativity of heteroatoms present in the BN-CDs. Also, for λex = 350 nm, PL intensity was found to be maximum (with λem =430 nm). Moreover, a bathochromic (blue to red) shift was observed for λem upon increasing the λex from 290 nm to 420 nm as shown in Fig. 5d, which was obtained upon normalizing the PL spectra of Fig. 5c.

Two popular photoluminescence mechanisms have been reported for carbon dots: one is based on the band gap transitions in the π-conjugation domains, and another is related to the surface defects in the carbon dots. In BN-CDs reported here, we believe that the surface state is the main factor controlling the PL variations because the peak was broadened with a similar particle size. Moreover, the PL redshift was due to the surface defects in the BN-CDs due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which serve as a capture center for excitons and give rise to the surface-related fluorescence [11]. The surfaces of the BN-CDs in the current work are undoubtedly responsible for the fluorescence, and the surface states regulate the PL characteristics [11]. This furthermore confirmed that the results FTIR, XPS, and Raman spectrum indicates the presence of an amorphous region in the surface and π-conjugated domains in their carbon cores of the BN-CDs with the PL redshift.

The quantum yield of BN-CDs was determined by using the reference point method. As a standard for BN-CDs, quinine sulfate in 0.1 N sulfuric acid was used. BN-CDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of 21.9 ± 2.7 % in water, while quinine sulfate exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of 54 % in 0.1 N sulfuric acid (Table S1). The quantum yield of BN-CDs obtained in this study was 21.9 ± 2.7 % and we compared the quantum yield of carbon dots derived from other carbon precursors by doping method, and their quantum yields are listed in Table S2. These results reveal that the BN-CDs used in this study have higher quantum yields due to the surface functional groups present in them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of BN-CDs’ photoluminescence stability and its application thereof in the analysis of auric ions

ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical stability of BN-CDs was evaluated by varying ionic strength, pH and continuous UV exposure. There was no significant change in the PL intensity upon change in the ionic strength by altering the concentration of KCl (0–2 M) as shown in Fig. 6a. Fig. 6b, illustrates the effect of pH on the photophysical properties of the BN-CDs. There was no significant change in the PL intensity upon increasing the PH from 1 to 10. However, there was a sharp fall in the PL intensity when the pH was raised to 11 and 12. Finally, temporal dependence of BN-CD’s fluorescence stability upon UV exposure was evaluated. Figure S1 showed that there was no significant effect of UV light (365 nm) on the stability of BN-CDs’ photoluminescence properties for at least an hour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of specificity and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para: We looked into the potential utility of BN-CDs for auric ions detection in water. Fig. 7a illustrates the specific quenching of fluorescence intensity only in the presence of auric ions when compared to other metal ions.

To determine the limit of detection (LoD), different amounts of auric ions (0–40 µM) were added to the aqueous BN-CDs solution to measure the PL intensity of BN-CDs. We have selected 350 nm as the excitation wavelength to minimize the impact of auric ions absorption. The photoluminescence of the BN-CDs solution was found to be sensitive to auric ions, as indicated by the gradual decrease in PL intensity upon an increase in Au3+ ions (0–40 μM) as shown in Fig. 7b. The change in fluorescence intensity (F\F0) was plotted against the different auric ion concentrations in Fig. 7c. The graph, which showed a linear relationship with a high correlation coefficient (0.9824), was used to compute the detection limit. The detection limit of auric ions, 3.1 ± 0.45 nM was found to be lower when compared to the results reported in the literature as shown in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism behind the PL quenching of BN-CDs’ in the presence of auric ions
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible PL quenching mechanisms of CDs upon interaction with metal ions as reported in literature are as follows: 1) reduction in the pH upon adding Au3+, a Lewis acid 2) Forester resonance energy transfer (FRET), 3) reduction in the electron transfer from BNCDs to Au3+, and finally 4) coordination-induced aggregation.

In this case, as previously demonstrated in Fig. 6b, PL intensity did not change much even at very low pH, (pH = 1), in contrast to the majority of previously reported CDs [49]. Thus, the extremely little acidity that the Au3+ ions delivered into the analytical system cannot account for the observed dramatic PL quenching of BN-CDs upon the addition of Au3+ ions. The lack of overlap between the emission and absorption spectra of BN-CDs and Au3+ ions make it simple to rule out the mechanism of FRET from excited BN-CDs to Au3+ ions. In case of reduction in the electron transfer, auric ions are reduced to their zero-valence state, forming gold nanoparticles (GNPs) that result in typical pink color due to the observation of surface-plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption [35]. However, in our case, we did not observe any peak due to SPR, which is shown in Figure S2.

Further, when considering the mechanisms of coordination-induced aggregation, we performed a Stern-Volmer study, wherein the BN-CDs’ fluorescence intensities before and after interaction with auric ions were determined. The concentration-dependent quenching data was fitted with the Stern-Volmer equation to determine the quenching mechanism.
(4) F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q] = 1 + Kqτ0[Q]
Where,
Q - Concentration of auric ions (0–40 µM)
F0 - Fluorescence intensities of BN-CDs in the presence of auric ions
F – Fluorescence intensities in the absence of auric ions
kq - Bimolecular quenching rate constant
KSV - Stern-Volmer quenching constant
τ 0 - Fluorescence lifetimes in the absence of auric ions

Fig. 8a displayed the plot of F0/F vs [Q], which showed a strong negative linear association (correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.98). The near-perfect linear correlation indicated that the observed fluorescence quenching is not a static-dynamic combination. Further, to understand the mechanism in detail, we performed a fluorescence lifetime quenching study in the presence and absence of different amounts of Au3+ ions. Fig. 8b demonstrates that the Au3+ ions do not quench the fluorescence lifespan of BN-CDs. The unchanged lifetime of fluorescence confirms the static quenching. Therefore, the results of the fluorescence lifetime analysis indicated a ground-state interaction between Au3+ ions and BN-CDs. BN-CDs are comprised of a rich electron cloud on their surface that can effectively donate the electron pairs to the vacant 5d orbital of Au3+. The 5d orbital of Au3+ is vacant as it readily participates in the chelating process by accepting the electrons to fill up the orbital. This fluorescence modulation was ascribed to static quenching because of the ground state complex emergence between the quencher (Au3+ ions) and BN-CDs. During complexation, the excited electrons get trapped by a quencher (Au3+ ions) and it makes a non-fluorescent complex. Figure S3 demonstrates the plausible electron transfer process of BN-CDs to the vacant 5d orbital of Au3+. The photo-induced electrons gathered in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) instead of returning to the ground state of the BN-CDs. The residing electrons in the metastable state got transferred to the vacant 5d orbital of the Au3+ by the captivation and formed the ground state complex resulting in the filling of the 5d orbital of the Au3+. After the complex formation, the excited electrons are unable to return to the ground state (HOMO) and resulting in fluorescence quenching [20]. We hypothesize that the BN-CDs might be directed to self-assemble in a certain way with the addition of Au3+ ions, much like H-aggregates (Quenching mechanism represented in Figure S4) [49]. Also, we assumed that the formation of coordination bonds between the trivalent gold ion and the surface amine groups of BN-CDs may facilitate the process of self-assembly like the previously reported work [46]. This was confirmed using TEM image analysis as shown in Fig. 8d. As a result of self-assembly, non-uniform gold nanoparticles were formed. Thus, a coordination-induced aggregation mechanism could be a plausible PL quenching mechanism when BN-CDs interacted with auric ions. XPS spectrum was used to further analyze the chemical composition of Au3+ ions reaction with BN-CDs. From the survey scan XPS spectra (Figure S5a in the Supporting Information), it is obvious that the peaks at ∼286.09 eV, ∼401.33 eV, ∼533.77 eV, ∼193.87 eV, and ∼90.19 eV corresponds to the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, B 1 s, and Au 4 f core levels, respectively [29,30]. The XPS survey results also showed that the Au3+ ions were reacted on the surface of the BN-CDs. The resolved Au 4 f peaks at 90.19 eV corresponds to Au3+ 4f5/2. The appearance of Au3+ validated the coordination induced aggregation by BN-CDs, which was further verified by Figure S5b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing efficiency of BN-CDs towards auric ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: After establishing BN-CDs as an efficient sensing system for the detection of auric ions in de-ionized water, it is imperative to prove their efficacy in real water samples. A variety of water samples, including seawater (Paradise Beach, Pondicherry, India), tap water (Katpadi, India), and river water (Thenpennai River, kottavoor, India), were collected. An aliquot of the solution was spiked with 0–20 nM of Au3+ ions, and the solution was examined to determine the presence of auric ions by comparing it with deionized water. All the real water samples with the presence of auric ions exhibited similar PL quenching behavior. BN-CDs were able to detect the spiked amount of auric ions (n = 3) very precisely as shown in Table 2. Thus, it is evident that the system detects auric ions precisely even in the presence of organics, salts, and minerals in various field water samples. Thus, BN-CDs can be a potential sensing platform for the detection of auric ions in real environmental samples.

DPPH assay was used to determine the antioxidant activity of BN-CDs. The antioxidant property of BN-CDs increases dose-dependently at increasing concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/ml). The BN-CDs’ radical scavenging percentage was found to be high (50 %) at the highest concentration of 100 μg/ml and relatively low (20 %) at the lowest concentration of 12.5 μg/ml, as shown in Fig. 9a. The antioxidant properties of BN-CDs could be due to the presence of hydroxyl groups that are present on the particle's surface as confirmed by FTIR data, shown in Fig. 3.

We used NIH-3T3 to test the cytotoxicity of our BN-CDs. Fig. 9b showed that the obtained BN-CDs are not toxic to the cells even at high doses (0.1 mg/ml, 6 h incubation time). The acquired results demonstrated the biocompatibility of the BN-CDs. Thus, BN-CDs can be a potential cellular imaging agent.

Further, to visualize the viable and dead cells separately, we performed a live dead cells assay. Most of the cells were found to be viable (green cells) with very few dead (red cells), confirming the biocompatibility of BN-CDs, as shown in Fig. 10.

For cellular imaging applications, BN-CDs are to be taken up prominently by cells. To demonstrate the same, we performed BN-CDs uptake studies on the NIH-3T3 cell line. The NIH-3T3 cells treated with BN-CDs showed no significant change in shape as shown in Fig. 11a–e. A strong green-colored photoluminescence on the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area of cells indicated that the BN-CDs penetrated the cells and maintained their fluorescent properties in the cellular environment. The results seem to suggest that the cellular uptake of BN-CDs is efficient, requiring only a short incubation time (6 hours). Also, it was found that both the cells could be stained using very low concentration of BN-CDs, which could be mainly due to its high quantum yield. Thus, BN-CDs could also have potential cellular imaging applications, which can be tapped and further in-depth research in this direction are warranted.

We fabricated a user-friendly microwave-assisted method to develop biocompatible and highly luminous boron/nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-CDs), which can specifically detect Au3+ ions. We further hope this approach might be recommended as a cost-effective and greener one for commercial preparation of highly luminescent carbon dots in the future. The exceptional sensing ability of the produced BN-CDs towards the Au3+ ions was demonstrated to be Au3+ ions-induced PL quenching with a few real field water samples. A lower limit of detection (LOD) of 3.1 ± 0.45 nM indicated a better system than that reported in the literature. The quenching observed in the detection process was found to be primarily due to coordination-induced aggregation mechanism. Moreover, BN-CDs were also found to have superior radical scavenging activity, excellent cytocompatibility, and cell imaging properties. Thus, prepared BN-CDs can have potential applications in the accurate detection of auric ions as well as in cell imaging applications. Due to the highly fluorescent and biocompatible nature of the BN-CDs, this can be employed in multifaceted applications in future.
